name: amazon-reviews-2023

env_axis.name: category
env_axis.shift_mechanism: cross-category transfer (Clothing→Beauty, CDs→Digital-Music, Toys→Video-Games); temporal shift orthogonal

ood_protocol.name: amazon-cross-category
ood_protocol.ref: Ni et al. 2019; Hou et al. 2024 (Amazon Reviews 2023)

facts: 571.54M reviews from 54.51M users across 48.19M items, May 1996 - Sept 2023; 33 categories.
hypotheses: Category-cluster is a better environment unit than raw 'category', because several of the 33 categories are near-duplicates (e.g. fashion spans Clothing, Shoes, and Bags).
Temporal shift within a category dominates cross-category shift, because platform and reviewer behaviour drift faster over 1996-2023 than item semantics differ across categories.
intuitions: Cross-comparisons between the 2018 and 2023 releases likely exist in the recommender-systems literature.
Cross-domain recommendation (CDR) leaderboards for 2024-25 probably already use this release.
The McAuley group likely has post-2023 releases extending this collection.
unknowns: Whether user activity linking categories carries genuine cross-domain signal or merely correlation (people who buy X also buy Y) cannot be determined from the data.
The 33 categories are Amazon's commercial taxonomy, not a semantic taxonomy — whether ML-relevant distinctions match commercial ones cannot be decided from the data.
epistemic_state.boundary: The dataset has strong temporal (T=3) and typed (Ty=3) axes but C=0, so it cannot show within-review compositional shift.

materialization_status: 6 of 33 categories materialized (All_Beauty, Books, Clothing_Shoes_and_Jewelry, Electronics, Toys_and_Games, CDs_and_Vinyl). 22 GB. Other 27 categories deferred — same URL pattern works (mcauleylab.ucsd.edu/public_datasets/data/amazon_2023/raw/review_categories/<Category>.jsonl.gz).
